vulnerabilities.title: initializeAlphaIndices Access Control Mismatch Permanently Blocks Generation Increment
vulnerabilities.summary: EntropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices() uses onlyOwner modifier, but TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls it. Since TraitForgeNft is set as allowedCaller (not owner), the call reverts. After 10,000 gen-1 tokens are minted, all subsequent minting permanently halts because _mintInternal triggers _incrementGeneration which reverts on the unauthorized call to initializeAlphaIndices.

description.desc: initializeAlphaIndices has `onlyOwner` modifier but is called by TraitForgeNft (which is `allowedCaller`, not owner)
Affected function: _incrementGeneration

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - Protocol-breaking: After generation 1 fills (10,000 tokens), no new tokens can ever be minted via mintToken or mintWithBudget. The _mintNewEntity function (used by forge()) also calls _incrementGeneration() when the generation fills, so forging can also be blocked at generation boundaries. The entire minting and generation progression system freezes permanently. While the owner could manually call initializeAlphaIndices() as a workaround, _incrementGeneration() would still revert on the next generation transition. The only permanent fix requires contract redeployment or transferring EntropyGenerator ownership to TraitForgeNft (losing admin control) or modifying the code.

vulnerabilities.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Non-Functional After Generation 1
vulnerabilities.summary: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as its while-loop condition. _tokenIds is a global monotonically increasing counter, while maxTokensPerGen is 10,000. After generation 1 completes (10,000 tokens), _tokenIds >= 10,000 and the loop never executes. mintWithBudget becomes permanently non-functional for all subsequent generations, refunding all ETH.

description.desc: while (budgetLeft >= mintPrice && _tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen) uses global _tokenIds instead of generationMintCounts[currentGeneration]

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Batch minting functionality (mintWithBudget) is permanently disabled after generation 1 completes. Users in generations 2-10 must mint one token at a time via mintToken, significantly degrading UX and increasing gas costs for users who want to mint multiple tokens. This affects 90% of the protocol's lifetime (generations 2-10 out of 10 total). No workaround exists without contract redeployment or migration.

vulnerabilities.title: Forger Off-by-One Allows One Extra Forge Beyond Intended Limit
vulnerabilities.summary: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` (inclusive), allowing listing when count equals potential. forgeWithListed then increments count without rechecking. A forger with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times: the last listing passes at count=N (N<=N is true), then forge increments to N+1.

description.desc: forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential uses <= instead of <; forgeWithListed line 121 increments without recheck

vulnerabilities.impact: Every forger token can forge one extra time per yearly reset period. For forgePotential=1 tokens, this doubles their forging capacity (2 instead of 1). For forgePotential=9 tokens, it's a ~11% increase. This inflates the entity supply, generates extra forging fees for forger owners, and dilutes the value of all existing tokens. The extra entities also have access to the NukeFund, increasing potential drainage. The bug is systemic and affects all forger tokens from deployment.

vulnerabilities.title: Entropy Slot Recycling Makes All Post-Gen-1 Token Traits Predictable and Repeated
vulnerabilities.summary: EntropyGenerator stores 770 slots × 13 values = 10,010 entropy values. getNextEntropy wraps currentSlotIndex back to 0 after reaching slot 769. With 10,000 tokens per generation and up to 10 generations (100,000 tokens), entropy values repeat cyclically after the first 10,010 mints. Tokens in later generations have identical entropy to gen-1 tokens, fully predictable by any observer.

description.desc: currentSlotIndex wraps to 0 when >= maxSlotIndex-1; entropySlots never rewritten between generations
Affected function: _incrementGeneration

vulnerabilities.impact: After the first 10,010 entropy values are consumed (roughly gen-1 + 10 gen-2 mints), all subsequent tokens receive recycled entropy. This means: (1) All token traits are fully predictable for an informed observer, (2) Attackers can cherry-pick high-nuke-factor tokens to drain NukeFund repeatedly, (3) The randomness/fairness guarantee of the trait generation system is completely broken for 9 out of 10 generations, (4) The getPublicEntropy function makes this information freely accessible to anyone. The economic impact is severe: an informed attacker can monopolize high-value tokens across all generations.

vulnerabilities.title: Excess ETH Permanently Locked in EntityForging Contract
vulnerabilities.summary: forgeWithListed checks `msg.value >= forgingFee` but only distributes exactly forgingFee (split between nukeFund and forger owner). Any excess ETH (msg.value - forgingFee) remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract with no withdrawal mechanism, receive function, or sweep function.

description.desc: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) with no refund of (msg.value - forgingFee); devFee and forgerShare sum to forgingFee, not msg.value

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct ETH loss for any user who overpays forging fees. The severity is MEDIUM because: (1) the loss requires user error (sending more than needed), but (2) there is zero recovery possibility, and (3) the fix is trivial (either require exact payment or refund excess). This is a genuine code defect regardless of whether exploitation is intentional.

vulnerabilities.title: Stale listedTokenIds After Cancellation Causes Incorrect Forging Listing Behavior
vulnerabilities.summary: _cancelListingForForging deletes listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] but does NOT clear listedTokenIds[tokenId]. This stale pointer causes issues: (1) _beforeTokenTransfer queries getListedTokenIds which returns non-zero for cancelled tokens, triggering unnecessary listing checks; (2) if a new listing reuses the same listing index, the stale pointer could cause cross-token interference.

description.desc: delete listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] without delete listedTokenIds[tokenId]; stale mapping persists
Affected function: _beforeTokenTransfer

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity. The defect is a genuine incomplete state cleanup that violates the invariant INV-STATE-005 (listedTokenIds[tokenId] should be 0 after cancellation). Impact: (1) Wasted gas on every transfer of a previously-listed-then-cancelled token due to unnecessary SLOAD operations in _beforeTokenTransfer. (2) State inconsistency that could confuse off-chain systems or frontends reading listedTokenIds. (3) The safety checks in _beforeTokenTransfer prevent functional errors. (4) Cross-token interference is not possible because listing indices never collide. The same defect pattern also exists in EntityTrading.sol where buyNFT and cancelListing delete the listing but not listedTokenIds.

vulnerabilities.title: Generation Counter Can Exceed maxGeneration Via _incrementGeneration
vulnerabilities.summary: _incrementGeneration does not check whether currentGeneration < maxGeneration before incrementing. If generation 10 (maxGeneration) fills up, _incrementGeneration would set currentGeneration to 11, which exceeds the intended maximum. Subsequent mints would occur in an out-of-bounds generation.

description.desc: _incrementGeneration increments currentGeneration without checking against maxGeneration

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. Tokens minted beyond maxGeneration (generation > 10) violate the protocol's core design constraint. These tokens have anomalous properties: they can be nuked for fund claims but cannot be forged (forge checks maxGeneration). The pricing curve continues uncapped. This creates inconsistent game mechanics and could affect economic balance. The defect requires all 100,000 tokens (10 generations × 10,000) to be minted first, plus resolution of the initializeAlphaIndices bug.

vulnerabilities.title: NukeFund receive() DoS via ETH Transfer Failure to devAddress/daoAddress
vulnerabilities.summary: NukeFund.receive() sends devShare ETH to one of three addresses (devAddress, owner(), daoAddress) based on airdrop state. Each transfer uses a require(success) check. If the recipient contract rejects ETH (e.g., no receive/fallback or gas-intensive callback), the entire receive() reverts. Since ALL minting revenue flows through NukeFund.receive(), this would permanently brick minting, trading, and forging.

description.desc: Three conditional branches all use require(success) after call{value: devShare}; failure in any branch reverts the entire receive()

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: Complete protocol halt. If any one of the three recipient addresses in NukeFund.receive() cannot accept ETH during its active branch, all minting, trading, and forging operations are blocked. This affects all protocol users and prevents any economic activity. The fund balance continues to be tracked internally (fund += remainingFund happens before the send), but the revert rolls back the entire transaction including the fund update, so no ETH actually enters the protocol. Recovery requires the owner to identify the issue and update the affected address via setDevAddress/setDaoAddress/transferOwnership.

vulnerabilities.title: TraitForgeNft whitelist Merkle leaf encoding mismatch can lock out intended whitelisted users (case/format sensitivity)
vulnerabilities.summary: TraitForgeNft computes leaf as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)), while the provided Merkle tree generator hashes addresses with keccak256(addressString) in JS. If address normalization differs (case checksum, string vs 20-byte encoding), proofs may fail for legitimate users, causing mint DoS during whitelist phase.

description.desc: leaf = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)) vs JS keccak256(addressString) leaf generation

vulnerabilities.impact: All whitelisted minting is blocked during the whitelist window. This is a launch-blocking DoS for the first 24 hours (per constructor: whitelistEndTime = block.timestamp + 24 hours). Users who were promised whitelist access cannot mint. Protocol revenue from the whitelist phase is lost. The owner can recover by updating the root hash or ending the whitelist early, but this damages trust and may cause community frustration.

vulnerabilities.title: Fully Predictable Entropy Enables Selective High-Value Token Minting for Maximum NukeFund Extraction
vulnerabilities.summary: All entropy values are deterministic (derived from block.number at batch-write time), stored once, and publicly readable via getPublicEntropy(). Combined with publicly readable currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex, any user can predict exactly which entropy the next mint will yield. An attacker can calculate all 10,010 entropy values, identify the positions of the highest-value tokens (high nuke factor, 999999 alpha), and time their mints to capture them. On Base (no public mempool), the atta

description.desc: getPublicEntropy is public view returning getEntropy for any slot/number. currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex are private but inferable. Entire entropy sequence is deterministic from block.number at write time.
Affected function: mintToken(bytes32[])
Affected function: nuke(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL impact on protocol fairness and NukeFund solvency. An informed attacker can: (1) Capture the 999999 alpha token, worth ~25% of NukeFund per nuke. (2) Cherry-pick the highest entropy tokens (e.g., 900000+) for initialNukeFactor of ~22500, each worth ~22.5% of remaining fund. (3) Systematically drain the NukeFund at the expense of uninformed participants who mint blindly. This fundamentally breaks the game's fairness assumption—that token traits are unpredictable at mint time.

vulnerabilities.title: Uninitialized ageMultiplier Nullifies Age-Based Nuke Factor Making NukeFund Exploitable Immediately
vulnerabilities.summary: NukeFund.ageMultiplier defaults to 0 and the deployment script never calls setAgeMultplier(). calculateAge() returns (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * 0) / 365 = 0 for all tokens. This means nuke factor is purely entropy/40, completely ignoring hold time. Users can nuke tokens immediately after minimumDaysHeld (3 days) for the same payout as holding for years, undermining the core hold-to-earn mechanic.

description.desc: calculateAge multiplies by ageMultiplier which is 0 by default: age = (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * ageMultiplier) / 365; ageMultiplier=0 makes entire expression 0

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The core economic mechanism of the protocol (age-based value accrual) is completely non-functional. Token hold time has zero effect on nuke payouts. High-entropy tokens can extract up to 25% of the NukeFund after only 3 days. This undermines the entire game theory of the protocol, where users are supposed to be incentivized to hold tokens longer for greater rewards. Early strategic nukers with high-entropy tokens can drain the fund before other users accumulate meaningful value.

vulnerabilities.title: No Access Control on writeEntropyBatch Functions Allows Adversarial Entropy Seeding
vulnerabilities.summary: writeEntropyBatch1(), writeEntropyBatch2(), and writeEntropyBatch3() have no access control. Anyone can call them. Entropy values are derived from block.number at call time. An attacker who calls these functions at a strategically chosen block controls the entire entropy sequence for all 10,010 tokens. Between contract deployment and the deployer's batch-write transactions, there is a window where an attacker can front-run the batch writes.

description.desc: writeEntropyBatch1 is public with no onlyOwner/onlyAllowedCaller modifier. Same for writeEntropyBatch2 (lines 64-76) and writeEntropyBatch3 (lines 79-94)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL impact on game integrity: (1) The attacker controls the entropy landscape for ALL 10,010 tokens, (2) The attacker knows which token positions have high nuke factors, forger roles, high forge potentials, (3) Combined with public getPublicEntropy(), any user can verify the attacker's entropy was used, but by then it's permanent, (4) The deployer has NO recourse—redeploying the entire system is the only fix, (5) If the deployer doesn't notice, the attacker can selectively mint the most valuable tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: Flawed Entropy Padding Destroys Traits for 10% of Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: The entropy padding logic appends zeros to the right instead of left-padding. This forces the performance factor to 0 for 10% of all tokens, making them completely worthless for age-based NukeFund claims.

description.desc: paddedEntropy = entropy * (10 ** (6 - numberOfDigits(entropy))) appends zeros to the right

vulnerabilities.impact: Approximately 10% of all minted tokens (those whose sliced entropy falls in the range 0-99999) will have a performance factor of 0 due to right-padding. These tokens cannot accrue age-based nuke fund value, making them significantly less valuable for the hold-to-earn mechanic. The economic impact is that uninformed users waste minting fees on tokens with permanently diminished nuke potential, while informed users can avoid them using getPublicEntropy. This creates an unfair information asymmetry and destroys approximately 10% of the intended token value generation.

vulnerabilities.title: Secondary Market Buyers Can Destroy Initial Minters' Airdrop Allocations
vulnerabilities.summary: When a token is burned (nuked), its entropy is subtracted from the original minter's airdrop allocation, even if the minter no longer owns the token. Buyers can intentionally nuke tokens to grief minters.

description.desc: airdropContract.subUserAmount(initialOwners[tokenId], entropy) penalizes the initial owner, not current owner

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH impact. Original minters who sell tokens before the airdrop face complete loss of their airdrop allocation for those tokens. This creates a perverse market dynamic where selling tokens is penalized, destroying secondary market liquidity and trust. A targeted attacker can strategically destroy whale minters' airdrop allocations to increase their own relative share of the fixed airdrop pool. The economic damage extends beyond individual victims to systemic market dysfunction.

vulnerabilities.title: Emergency Pause Mechanism Completely Non-Functional Across All Contracts
vulnerabilities.summary: All six contracts (TraitForgeNft, EntityForging, EntityTrading, NukeFund, EntropyGenerator, DevFund) inherit OpenZeppelin Pausable and use whenNotPaused modifiers on critical functions. However, none expose external pause() or unpause() functions. OpenZeppelin's _pause() and _unpause() are internal, requiring wrapper functions. Without them, the pause mechanism is dead code—the protocol has no emergency brake. If a vulnerability is discovered in production, the owner cannot halt operations.

description.desc: Contract inherits Pausable, uses whenNotPaused on mintToken/burn/forge, but no function calling _pause() or _unpause() exists anywhere in the contract
Affected function: N/A - missing pause/unpause
Affected function: N/A - missing pause/unpause
Affected function: N/A - missing pause/unpause
Affected function: N/A - missing pause/unpause
Affected function: N/A - missing pause/unpause

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - The protocol has no emergency brake. All six contracts have whenNotPaused modifiers on critical financial functions (minting, trading, forging, nuking, claiming) but these guards can never activate. This means: (1) any future exploit cannot be stopped, (2) the team has zero emergency response capability, (3) the issue is permanent from deployment and cannot be fixed without redeploying all contracts and migrating state. The impact is amplified because it affects every contract in the protocol simultaneously.

vulnerabilities.title: NukeFund claim cap can be bypassed by parameter combination (nukeFactorMaxParam vs maxAllowedClaimDivisor) leading to >50% fund claims
vulnerabilities.summary: In NukeFund.nuke, claimAmount is only capped to fund/maxAllowedClaimDivisor when finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam. If finalNukeFactor is high (e.g., 60,000–99,999) but nukeFactorMaxParam is configured higher than it, the cap does not apply and potentialClaimAmount can exceed the intended maxAllowedClaimAmount (e.g., >50% when divisor=2). A patient holder can then drain a disproportionate share in one nuke.

description.desc: claimAmount = finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam ? maxAllowedClaimAmount : potentialClaimAmount; (not min())

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is HIGH when parameter misalignment occurs: a single nuke operation can drain more than the intended maximum percentage of the fund. With nukeFactorMaxParam=80000 and maxAllowedClaimDivisor=2, up to 80% of the fund can be claimed in one nuke instead of the intended 50%. With nukeFactorMaxParam above MAX_DENOMINATOR, the entire fund (and more, causing revert) could be attempted. The ETH payout is irreversible.

vulnerabilities.title: Strategic Forging to Produce Nuke-Optimized Offspring with Predictable High Entropy
vulnerabilities.summary: Forged entity entropy is calculated as (forgerEntropy + mergerEntropy) / 2, and both parent entropies are publicly readable. An attacker can select parent pairs whose averaged entropy maximizes the child's nuke factor (initialNukeFactor = entropy/40), then nuke the offspring to extract disproportionate ETH from the NukeFund. The forging cost (minimum 0.01 ETH fee) can be far less than the nuke payout.

description.desc: uint256 newEntropy = (forgerEntropy + mergerEntropy) / 2; fully deterministic from public state
Affected function: nuke(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH. If NukeFund holds 100 ETH, each optimized forge-nuke cycle extracts ~20 ETH for ~0.001 ETH net cost. With forgePotential allowing multiple forges per parent pair, an attacker could drain a significant portion of the NukeFund in days. All other NukeFund participants (holding lower-entropy tokens) suffer diluted payouts as the fund is systematically drained. The attack's effectiveness scales with fund size.

vulnerabilities.title: DevFund Integer Division Truncation Routes Small Payments Entirely to Owner
vulnerabilities.summary: DevFund.receive() calculates amountPerWeight = msg.value / totalDevWeight. When msg.value < totalDevWeight, amountPerWeight truncates to 0 and the entire msg.value is sent to the owner as 'remaining'. With deployment's totalDevWeight=300, any payment under 300 wei has amountPerWeight=0. While individual losses are tiny, systematic truncation across thousands of small transactions accumulates. The truncation also applies to the remainder on every transaction regardless of size.

description.desc: amountPerWeight = msg.value / totalDevWeight; remaining = msg.value - (amountPerWeight * totalDevWeight); remaining sent to owner

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is real but has negligible practical impact for normal protocol operations. The maximum truncation per transaction is 299 wei. For the sub-300-wei edge case, the payment goes entirely to the owner, but such small payments are extremely unlikely in normal protocol operation since devShare from minting starts at 0.0005 ETH. The missing precision multiplier is a genuine code quality issue compared to the standard MasterChef accumulator pattern.

vulnerabilities.title: EntityTrading listNFTForSale Resets Nuke Maturity Timer via Transfer Hook
vulnerabilities.summary: When listing an NFT for sale via EntityTrading.listNFTForSale, the token is transferred from owner to EntityTrading contract. This triggers _beforeTokenTransfer which resets lastTokenTransferredTimestamp to block.timestamp. When the listing is cancelled (cancelListing), the token transfers back, resetting the timestamp again. Any user who lists and cancels a token loses their accumulated nuke maturity progress (minimumDaysHeld = 3 days). Buying also resets the timer for the new owner.

description.desc: if (from != to) { lastTokenTransferredTimestamp[firstTokenId] = block.timestamp; } fires on every transfer including listing/delisting
Affected function: listNFTForSale(uint256,uint256)
Affected function: canTokenBeNuked(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: Users who list and cancel NFTs on EntityTrading lose up to 3 days of accumulated nuke maturity. Users who buy NFTs always start with a fresh maturity timer. This creates an unexpected penalty for using the trading system and may trap users in repeated cycles of delayed nuke eligibility. The economic impact is the time-value-of-money from delayed nuke payouts and potentially missed optimal nuke timing windows when the NukeFund balance is high.

vulnerabilities.title: Forging listing cancellation can permanently brick ALL NFT transfers if EntityForging is unset or miswired
vulnerabilities.summary: TraitForgeNft._beforeTokenTransfer() unconditionally calls into entityForgingContract (getListedTokenIds / getListings) for every transfer. If entityForgingContract is the zero address or a non-contract address, these external calls will revert, bricking transfers/mints/burns that rely on the hook.

description.desc: _beforeTokenTransfer calls entityForgingContract.getListedTokenIds() and getListings() without checking entityForgingContract != address(0) or code length

vulnerabilities.impact: If entityForgingContract is misconfigured, the entire NFT protocol is bricked: no minting, no burning, no transferring, no forging, no trading, no nuking. All user funds in NukeFund remain locked (nuke requires burn which requires _beforeTokenTransfer). EntityTrading listings cannot be cancelled (requires transferFrom which requires _beforeTokenTransfer). The owner can recover by calling setEntityForgingContract with the correct address, but users cannot self-recover. The impact is protocol-wide DoS affecting all NFT holders.

vulnerabilities.title: EntityTrading listings can be overwritten/relisted without clearing listedTokenIds, enabling stale-index state and unexpected behavior
vulnerabilities.summary: EntityTrading deletes listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] on buy and cancel but never clears listedTokenIds[tokenId]. This creates stale pointers similar to EntityForging. Subsequent interactions that rely on listedTokenIds (including off-chain indexers/UI, or future functions) can misinterpret listing existence, and a relist will overwrite listedTokenIds[tokenId] but listingCount continues to grow, allowing confusing states.

description.desc: delete listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] without delete listedTokenIds[tokenId]

vulnerabilities.impact: Moderate severity code defect. The incomplete state cleanup creates mapping inconsistency between listedTokenIds and listings. Current on-chain functions are protected by secondary checks on the listing struct itself, but the public mapping provides incorrect information to external consumers. This is a genuine missing-cleanup bug that breaks the expected invariant: after buy/cancel, listedTokenIds[tokenId] should be 0.

vulnerabilities.title: TraitForgeNft mint flows can be DoSed by malicious/buggy NukeFund (or unset nukeFundAddress) because funds are forwarded via raw call
vulnerabilities.summary: TraitForgeNft._distributeFunds forwards ETH to nukeFundAddress using low-level call and requires success. If nukeFundAddress is unset (zero address) or points to a contract that reverts on receive/fallback, minting will revert, halting protocol revenue and user onboarding.

description.desc: nukeFundAddress.call{ value: totalAmount }(''); require(success)

vulnerabilities.impact: If nukeFundAddress is address(0): all minting revenue is silently burned (permanent fund loss, not DoS). If nukeFundAddress is a reverting contract: complete minting halt until owner fixes via setNukeFundContract. The cascading revert path through NukeFund.receive() (where devAddress/daoAddress/owner rejecting ETH bricks minting) expands the attack surface. Recovery is possible if owner retains access to call setNukeFundContract.

vulnerabilities.title: EntityTrading buyNFT can revert (and lock listed NFTs in escrow) if nukeFundAddress is unset or rejects ETH
vulnerabilities.summary: EntityTrading.buyNFT always calls transferToNukeFund which requires nukeFundAddress != 0 and requires the call succeed. If misconfigured, purchases revert. Since NFTs are escrowed in EntityTrading during listing, a broken buy flow can strand NFTs in the contract until seller cancels (if cancel still works).

description.desc: transferToNukeFund requires nukeFundAddress != address(0) and requires low-level call success

vulnerabilities.impact: If nukeFundAddress is unset or rejects ETH: all marketplace purchases fail, sellers with escrowed NFTs cannot complete sales, marketplace liquidity is frozen. Recovery requires either owner fixing the address or each seller individually cancelling their listings. cancelListing remains functional as it does not depend on nukeFundAddress. The pause mechanism cannot be triggered (no external pause/unpause exposed per INV-ACCESS-001), so if additional issues block cancelListing, NFTs could be permanently stranded.

vulnerabilities.title: Mint Price Plummets on Generation Increment Enabling Arbitrage
vulnerabilities.summary: When a new generation starts, the mint count resets to 0, causing the mint price to drop from its maximum (~0.25 ETH) back to the base startPrice (0.005 ETH), creating a massive arbitrage opportunity.

description.desc: priceIncrease = priceIncrement * currentGenMintCount; resets to 0 when generationMintCounts[currentGeneration] resets

vulnerabilities.impact: No impact from a code defect perspective. The pricing behavior is intentional. While early minters in each generation get a price advantage, this is a deliberate game design feature, not a security vulnerability. The priceIncrementByGen mechanism (which slightly increases the slope each generation) shows the developers balanced this price reset with gradually steeper curves.

vulnerabilities.title: Forger ForgePotential TOCTOU: Year Reset Between Listing and Forging Bypasses Limit
vulnerabilities.summary: forgeWithListed does not re-validate the forger's forgePotential at forge time. It only increments forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] without checking against forgePotential. The check was done at listing time in listForForging, but _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called in forgeWithListed which can reset forgingCounts to 0 if a year has passed. This creates a TOCTOU: forger lists at count=forgePotential (allowed by off-by-one), a year passes, forgeWithListed resets count to 0 and increments to 1. The f

description.desc: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without re-checking against forgePotential; preceded by _resetForgingCountIfNeeded which may reset count to 0

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is real: forgePotential is not re-validated at forge time, and the code comment explaining the omission is incorrect. The asymmetric treatment (merger is checked, forger is not) further confirms this is unintentional. However, the practical impact is LIMITED because: (1) each forge consumes the listing (cancelled), (2) the year reset allows fresh listing with count=0 anyway, (3) the total forges per year cycle remains forgePotential+1 (due to off-by-one) regardless of stale listings. The TOCTOU enables the same number of forges achievable through normal re-listing after year reset, making exploitation economically equivalent to normal usage.

vulnerabilities.title: Airdrop Contract Failure in burn() Can Permanently Lock NukeFund ETH
vulnerabilities.summary: NukeFund.nuke() calls nftContract.burn(tokenId). Inside burn(), if airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false, it calls airdropContract.subUserAmount(). If this external call reverts (buggy airdrop contract, gas limits, or ownership transfer issues), the entire nuke transaction reverts. Fund balance is deducted before burn in nuke(), but since it's atomic, the revert restores it. However, if this condition persists, NO tokens can be nuked, permanently locking all NukeFund ETH.

description.desc: burn() calls airdropContract.subUserAmount without try/catch; revert propagates to caller (nuke)
Affected function: nuke(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - If triggered, all nuke operations across the entire protocol are blocked. The NukeFund accumulates ETH from all minting (90% of mint fees), trading, and forging activities. With no ability to nuke, this ETH becomes inaccessible to all NFT holders. The mitigation path (starting the airdrop) exists but requires owner intervention and may have its own prerequisites or side effects. The core defect is the missing error handling on an external call in a critical code path.

vulnerabilities.title: NukeFund Accounting Mismatch Risk: Internal `fund` Can Diverge From Actual ETH Balance via Forced ETH or Bypassed Receive Logic
vulnerabilities.summary: NukeFund uses an internal `fund` variable updated only in receive(). ETH can be forced into the contract (e.g., via selfdestruct) or transferred through paths that do not execute receive() (or later EVM changes), creating a divergence between address(this).balance and fund. Operationally this can distort payout expectations, solvency monitoring, and cap calculations (claimAmount uses `fund`, not actual balance). Attackers can also grief by forcing ETH to create misleading solvency signals or man

description.desc: Uses private `fund` accounting updated only in receive(); claim logic uses `fund` not address(this).balance

vulnerabilities.impact: No on-chain impact. The internal `fund` accounting is correct and more secure than using `address(this).balance`. The trapped forced ETH represents permanently locked value with no recovery mechanism, but this is an accepted tradeoff of the secure accounting pattern. The maximum griefing impact is wasting the attacker's own ETH by selfdestructing it into NukeFund.
